retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Pharyngitis -- Pathophysiology -- Group A β-Hemolytic Streptococcus
retrieved_docs.content: All GABS strains secrete streptokinase and have plasminogen binding sites on the surface. When plasminogen binds, streptokinase converts plasminogen to plasmin and interrupts the coagulation cascade, aiding the spread of infection to deep tissues. [17] Streptokinase is also involved in the development of poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis. Some strains secrete S treptococcus pyogenes nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotidase, which is translocated into the host cells to deplete nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide stores. See StatPearls' companion reference, " Poststreptococcal Glomerulonephritis ," for additional information regarding the pathophysiology of poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.

retrieved_docs.title: Group A Streptococcal Infections -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: GAS infections result from a complex interplay between host and bacterial factors that facilitate the establishment of infection. GAS utilizes various virulence factors, including toxins and other substances, to evade the host immune system and infect humans. [1] The hyaluronic acid capsule of GAS acts as a camouflage mechanism by resembling human hyaluronic acid, allowing the bacterium to evade immune detection. The surface-associated protein (S-protein) protects the bacterium from phagocytic destruction. Additionally, GAS produces proteases that degrade host immune signaling molecules and extracellular DNases to neutralize host immune defenses. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The virulence factors which assist anaerobic infections are adhesions factors (fimbriae and lectin), invasion factors (phospholipase C, lipopolysaccharides, and proteases), factors involved in tissue destruction (fibrinolysis, acetylglucosaminidase, and collagenase production), capsular resistance to phagocytosis, and others. Serious infections are seen in the immunocompromised host.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Pharyngitis -- Pathophysiology -- Group A β-Hemolytic Streptococcus
retrieved_docs.content: GABS has a number of virulence factors that subvert the host's natural immune response. Cell surface proteins, hemolytic toxins, pyrogenic exotoxins, and bacterial structural components all contribute to its virulence. M and M-like proteins help the organism evade phagocytosis and complement deposition. [15] Toxins like streptolysin O further exacerbate tissue damage by affecting platelets and endothelial cells, leading to ischemic injury. [16] Streptolysin S lyses red blood cells. Hyaluronidase aids in infection spread along fascial planes by breaking down hyaluronic acid in connective tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Group A Streptococcal Infections -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Various surface substances, such as lipoteichoic acid and F-protein, enable GAS to adhere to host cells and facilitate colonization. [1] Cytolytic toxins, including streptolysins and hyaluronidase, contribute to tissue destruction, allowing GAS to invade the host. [1] [43] Additionally, GAS has several factors, such as its capsule, G-protein, C5a peptidase, and M-protein, to evade host immune defenses. Among these, M-protein is particularly significant for GAS virulence, as it inhibits the phagocytosis of host immune cells. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Group B Streptococcus and Pregnancy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: At the cellular level, GBS regulates its virulence factors through signal transduction systems, allowing it to adapt and survive in various host environments beyond the lower genital tract. The most studied system is CovR/S, which regulates many virulence genes. GBS also controls virulence using one-component transcriptional regulators and a serine/threonine kinase system. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Clostridial Myositis and Myonecrosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha toxin is essential to the development of gas gangrene. A hemolytic toxin, it has both phospholipase C and sphingomyelinase activity. Alpha toxin also stimulates platelet aggregation and upregulates adherence molecules on neutrophils and endothelial cells. This leads to the formation of occlusive intravascular aggregates composed of activated platelets, leukocytes, and fibrin which causes a rapid, irreversible decline in muscle blood flow and ischemic necrosis of tissue. Decreased perfusion promotes the development of the anaerobic environment and contributes to the rapidly advancing margins of tissue destruction characteristic of clostridial gas gangrene. Alpha toxin is also responsible for the characteristic absence of tissue inflammatory response. As previously mentioned, large aggregates of activated platelets and neutrophils reduce the ability of neutrophils to cross the endothelial cell barrier into infected tissues. This is accomplished by alpha toxin-induced activation of the platelet fibrinogen receptor, glycoprotein IIb/IIIa. Alpha and theta toxins also are cytotoxic to neutrophils; the toxins most likely destroy the small number of leukocytes that do successfully migrate into tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Corynebacterium Diphtheriae -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Still, the toxigenic strains of these bacteria do produce toxins that are then released into the circulation resulting wide range of clinical manifestations. Exotoxin is released by endosomes, causes a localized inflammatory reaction followed by necrosis and tissue destruction. The toxin is made of two joined proteins. The B fragment binds to a receptor on the surface of susceptible host cells, which cleaves the membrane lipid layer enabling segment A to enter. [6] Fragment A inhibits an amino acid transfer, thus inhibiting protein synthesis. Diphtheria toxin (DT) causes a catalytic transfer of NAD to diphthamide, which inactivates the elongation factor that results in protein synthesis blockade and, ultimately, cell death. [7] Local tissue destruction enables the toxin to be carried by blood and lymph to other parts of the body. Elaboration of the diphtheria toxin may affect the myocardium, kidneys, and nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Pathophysiology -- Infectious Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of infectious diseases often involves hijacking cellular receptors or altering their function. For example, viruses frequently gain entry into host cells by binding to specific cellular receptors. Similarly, some bacterial toxins target specific receptors to exert their effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Group A Streptococcal Infections -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous virulence determinants in GAS have been identified, aiding in adhesion, colonization, evasion of the innate immune system, invasion, and dissemination within the host. [22] Key virulence factors include the M-protein, hyaluronic acid, streptokinase, and deoxyribonuclease (DNase)-B. Among its notable toxins are the pyrogenic toxins (also known as scarlatina or erythrogenic toxins), which are responsible for the rash in scarlet fever. These toxins also stimulate mononuclear cells to produce tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-alpha and interleukins (IL-1 and IL-6), thereby contributing to fever and shock in patients with STSS. [1] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Streptococcus Pyogenes -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple virulence factors are responsible for clinical manifestations of the S. pyogenes . Bacterial capsule consisted of hyaluronic acid provides protection against phagocytosis. M protein, lipoteichoic acid, and protein F are responsible for the attachment of the bacteria to host cells. M protein is also responsible for inhibiting opsonization by binding to complement regulators and to fibrinogen. M protein is the most important virulence factor for S. pyogenes since experiments have shown that M mutants cannot survive in phagocyte-containing human blood. [11] S. pyogenes also produce exotoxins, like a pyrogenic (erythrogenic) toxin, which is responsible for the rash of scarlet fever and toxic shock syndrome. Other virulent factors include streptokinase, streptodornase, hyaluronidase, and streptolysins, which help in the invasion of tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Group B Streptococcus and Pregnancy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ssr1/Ssr2: Ssr2, found in clonal complex 17 strains, has a greater binding affinity to fibrinogen and can bind plasminogen, aiding in dissemination. HvgA: Found in clonal complex 17 strains, HvgA enhances adherence to intestinal and brain cells, promoting colonization and crossing of barriers. FbsA, FbsB, FbsC: These fibrinogen-binding proteins are crucial for adherence, biofilm formation, and dissemination. Lmb: Binds laminin, aiding in tissue invasion and neurotropism. C5a peptidase: Facilitates immune evasion and adherence. Pili: Cell wall–anchored structures that assist in adherence and resistance to immune mechanisms. PbsP: Promotes vaginal colonization and invasive infection. SfbA: Enhances invasion of various cells and contributes to brain colonization. BibA: A multifunctional protein involved in colonization and immune evasion. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Group B Streptococcus and Pregnancy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: GBS also produces a hemolytic pigment regulated by the CovR/S system, which aids in colonization, tissue damage, and immune evasion. Other virulence factors include superoxide dismutase for resisting reactive oxygen species, hyaluronidase for tissue invasion, and a sialic acid–rich capsular polysaccharide for immune evasion by mimicking maternal protein structures or molecular mimicry. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Clostridium perfringens Infection -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: ITX is a binary toxin produced as two distinct proteins, Ia and Ib. Ib binds to a cell surface receptor and associates with Ia. The complex is then endocytosed. Ia will pass to the cytosol through a membrane channel created by Ib and then depolymerize the actin cytoskeleton via ADP-ribosylation. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Haemophilus influenzae Infection -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Haemophilus influenzae is characterized as a small (0.3 micrometer to 1 micrometer), facultatively anaerobic, pleomorphic, and capnophilic gram-negative coccobacillus of the family Pasteurellaceae . It requires a medium containing two erythrocyte factors, i.e., factor X (hematin) and factor V (phos­phopyridine nucleotide), which are released after lysis of red blood cells, thereby favoring the growth of this organism on chocolate agar. Some of H. influenzae have a polysaccharide capsule depending upon which they are classified into 6 distinct groups designated as serotypes a, b, c, d, e, and f. These serotypes are detected based on the agglutination reaction to serum specific to them. H. influenzae type b (Hib) is noteworthy because of its polyribosyl ribitol phosphate (PRP) capsule, which accounts for 95% of invasive disease in children and >50% of invasive disease in adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Complement -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: This regulatory plasma glycoprotein can be used by pathogens and cancer cells to evade the immune system. [19] [20] Specific pathogens using this mechanism of evasion include Haemophilus influenzae , Streptococcus pneumoniae , Borrelia burgdorferi , Staphylococcus aureus , West Nile Virus, and HIV-1. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is classified as a member of the hematological enzymes group, reflecting its role in blood‑related enzymatic processes. It is derived from the endogenous protease plasmin, indicating that its molecular identity is mapped from plasmin. In clinical applications, Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin serves as the active ingredient of the human fibrinolysin preparation, underscoring its therapeutic function in promoting fibrinolysis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin, a therapeutic agent that promotes fibrinolysis by breaking down fibrin clots. By incorporating the enzymatic activities of both deoxyribonucleases and plasmin, this formulation leverages plasmin’s natural ability to cleave fibrin and deoxyribonucleases’ capacity to degrade nucleic acids, enhancing the overall efficacy of fibrinolysin in clinical settings where clot dissolution is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1149
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin corresponds to the enzyme plasmin and serves as the active ingredient in the human fibrinolysin medication. The drug formulation of human fibrinolysin relies on this plasmin-derived component to promote fibrinolysis, and the entity Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is directly mapped from the native plasmin protein.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 709
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is identified as the active ingredient of the human fibrinolysin preparation, indicating its therapeutic use in fibrinolytic therapy. This enzyme is derived from, or closely related to, plasmin, reflecting its origin in the physiological fibrinolytic pathway. Classified within the category of hematological enzymes, Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin belongs to the broader group “Enzymes, hematological,” which in turn is a subset of the larger enzyme family termed deoxyribonukleasy. Within this family, streptodornase represents a more narrowly defined enzyme, highlighting the hierarchical organization of these nucleic‑acid‑degrading proteins. Understanding these relationships clarifies how Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin fits into both clinical applications and the broader taxonomy of hematological and deoxyribonuclease enzymes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 564
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is the active ingredient of the human fibrinolysin preparation and is classified as a member of the broader group of hematological enzymes. Within this same Enzymes, hematological category, deoxyribonukleasy is also included, and it has a more specific relationship to streptodornase, indicating that streptodornase is a particular type of deoxyribonuclease. Hyaluronoglukosaminidasa likewise belongs to the Enzymes, hematological class and is further placed in the therapeutic class Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers [TC], reflecting its use as a therapeutic enzyme replacement. These interconnections demonstrate how these enzymes are organized by functional family, therapeutic application, and specificity, providing clinicians with a clear framework for selecting and understanding enzyme‑based treatments in hematological and related medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglobulin, blood coagulation factors, and protein hydrolytic enzymes are all entities for which ultrastructural characteristics are recognized as valid descriptors; the ultrastructure of each of these proteins can be examined to elucidate their detailed molecular organization and functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Antithrombotic enzymes constitute a class of therapeutic proteins that act to dissolve blood clots, and several specific agents are recognized as members of this group. Streptokinase, tenecteplase, urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, and brinase are all classified as antithrombotic enzymes, indicating that each of these agents shares the common functional property of promoting fibrinolysis. By belonging to the same enzyme class, these drugs are linked by their mechanism of activating plasminogen or directly degrading fibrin, which is central to their use in the treatment and prevention of thrombotic disorders. Understanding that streptokinase, tenecteplase, urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, and brinase are all members of the antithrombotic enzyme family helps clinicians select appropriate fibrinolytic therapy and appreciate the shared pharmacologic basis of these treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 616
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is classified as a hematological enzyme, belonging to the broader enzyme group Enzymes, hematological, which in turn is a subset of the larger family deoxyribonukleasy. Within deoxyribonukleasy, more specific members such as streptodornase are recognized, indicating that streptodornase represents a narrower subclass of these nucleic‑acid‑degrading enzymes. Clinically, Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of the human fibrinolysin preparation, highlighting its therapeutic role in promoting fibrinolysis. This hierarchical and functional information links the enzyme’s biochemical classification to its practical application in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: therefore includes plasmin and its activators (urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, anistreplase, streptokinase, reteplase, tPA, tenecteplase), which exhibit proteolytic, brinase‑type activity. Human fibrinolysin produces deoxyribonucleases/plasmin, illustrating the therapeutic application of plasmin‑derived molecules. Heparin‑group antithrombotic drugs such as enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin potentiate antithrombin III, thereby augmenting inhibition of thrombin and factor Xa and providing complementary anticoagulation to fibrinolytic enzymes. Together, the classification of fibrinolytic and heparin‑based antithrombotic agents, the role of protein S, and the integration of contrast‑enhanced fluoroscopic imaging form a comprehensive framework for the safe and effective management of arterial and venous thrombi across diverse clinical and interventional settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 475
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin serves as the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin and is classified as a hematological enzyme. Hematological enzymes are recognized as members of the broader deoxyribonukleasy group while also being included in the trypsin family, reflecting an overlapping enzymatic classification. Within the deoxyribonukleasy hierarchy, streptodornase represents a more specific enzyme subtype. This integrated categorization clarifies the functional relationships and therapeutic relevance of Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin, Fibrinolysin human, Enzymes hematological, deoxyribonukleasy, trypsin, and streptodornase in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: Enzyme agents encompass a range of specific antithrombotic enzymes, including urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, anistreplase, reteplase, streptokinase, and tissue plasminogen activator. These individual agents are classified as members of the broader group of antithrombotic enzymes, which also includes tenecteplase. Consequently, the various enzyme agents serve as constituent members of the antithrombotic enzyme class, highlighting their shared role in fibrinolysis and thrombus dissolution.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 601
retrieved_docs.content: Enzyme agent functions as an activator of plasminogen of the urokinase type and includes reteplase as its child, a recombinant urokinase‑type plasminogen activator used clinically for thrombolytic therapy.

retrieved_docs.content: The pharmacologic effect of these procedures relies on antithrombotic enzymes of the plasmin family; deoxyribonucleases/Plasmin derived from plasmin serves as the active component of human fibrinolysin, while urokinase‑type plasminogen activator, anistreplase, reteplase, streptokinase, tissue plasminogen activator, and tenektepláza convert plasminogen to plasmin to achieve fibrin degradation. Fibrinolytika are excluded from endovascular intracranial prolonged administration of non‑thrombolytic agents, and accidental poisoning by fibrinolytics is recognized as a distinct clinical condition, emphasizing the need for careful handling and monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 712
retrieved_docs.content: Antithrombotic enzymes constitute a therapeutic class that includes plasmin, streptokinase, protein C, tenektepláza, anistreplase and brinase, each of which is considered a member of this group. By promoting fibrinolysis or inhibiting coagulation pathways, these enzymes function to dissolve existing clots and prevent new thrombus formation, making them valuable agents in the management of thrombotic disorders.
